name: Dinka

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Dinka language
sources.notes: Consonant inventory (five places, no fricatives except /ɣ/), 13 short vowels with modal/breathy contrast, three contrastive lengths.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Dinka alphabet
sources.notes: Letter-by-letter IPA table used for this grapheme map: b c d dh g ɣ j k l m n nh ny ŋ p r t th w y; vowels a ä e ë ɛ ɛ̈ i ï o ö ɔ ɔ̈ u with the diaeresis marking breathy voice; long vowels written by doubling; f q s v x z unused, h only in digraphs.

sources.author: Remijsen, Bert & Gilley, Leoma
sources.title: Why are three-level vowel length systems rare? Insights from Dinka (Luanyjang dialect)
sources.notes: On the length/voice-quality/tone system the orthography under-marks.

sources.author: Remijsen, Bert & Manyang, Caguor Adong
sources.title: Luanyjang Dinka (Illustrations of the IPA)
sources.publisher: Journal of the International Phonetic Association 39(1)
sources.notes: 20 consonants, seven vowel qualities /i e ɛ a ɔ o u/, three lengths and a modal/breathy contrast; states that the article's transcription (1/2/3 vowel characters for short/mid/long, tone and breathiness as diacritics) is a phonetic convention and NOT a proposal to modify the existing orthography — i.e. tone is not written in the standard orthography.

sources.author: Government of the Anglo-Egyptian Sudan
sources.title: Report of the Rejaf Language Conference, 1928
sources.notes: Conference that adopted the shared southern-Sudanese Latin orthography from which the modern Dinka alphabet derives.

notes: Dinka (Thuɔŋjäŋ) — Western Nilotic language of South Sudan. INPUT CONTRACT: the standard South Sudanese Dinka Latin orthography (the Rek-based standard written variety, descended from the alphabet adopted at the 1928 Rejaf Language Conference and shared across the Dinka dialects). Input is assumed to be already in this orthography with its special letters ⟨ɛ ɔ ɣ ŋ⟩ and diaeresis vowels ⟨ä ë ï ö ɛ̈ ɔ̈⟩; ad-hoc ASCII substitutes (e.g. 'e' for 'ɛ', 'ng' for 'ŋ') are OUT OF SCOPE and must be normalized upstream. KEY FEATURES: (1) DENTAL vs ALVEOLAR: a following ⟨h⟩ marks the dental series — ⟨th⟩ = /t̪/, ⟨dh⟩ = /d̪/, ⟨nh⟩ = /n̪/. These are STOPS, not fricatives: Dinka has no fricatives other than /ɣ/. ⟨h⟩ occurs only in these digraphs. (2) PALATALS: ⟨c⟩ = /c/, ⟨j⟩ = /ɟ/, ⟨ny⟩ = /ɲ/, ⟨y⟩ = /j/. ⟨ŋ⟩ = /ŋ/, ⟨ɣ⟩ = /ɣ/, ⟨r⟩ = tap /ɾ/. The letters f, q, s, v, x, z are not used. (3) VOWELS: seven qualities /i e ɛ a ɔ o u/; the DIAERESIS marks the breathy-voice series, transcribed here with the IPA breathy-voice diacritic (⟨ä⟩ = /a̤/, ⟨ë⟩ = /e̤/, ⟨ɛ̈⟩ = /ɛ̤/, ⟨ï⟩ = /i̤/, ⟨ö⟩ = /o̤/, ⟨ɔ̈⟩ = /ɔ̤/). ⟨u⟩ has no written breathy counterpart. (4) LENGTH: written by DOUBLING the vowel letter (⟨aa⟩ = /aː/, e.g. baai 'home'); doubled diaeresis vowels combine both conventions (⟨ëë⟩ = /e̤ː/). LIMITS OF RECOVERABILITY: (a) Dinka contrasts THREE vowel lengths (short, mid, long), but the standard orthography writes at most a two-way single/doubled distinction, so the short-vs-mid contrast is NOT recoverable from spelling; the map emits short for a single letter and long for a doubled letter. (b) LEXICAL TONE (and the tonal morphology of number/case marking) is NOT written in the standard orthography and is therefore NOT recoverable; no tone is emitted. (c) Creaky/modal-vs-breathy marking is written only via the diaeresis series and is applied inconsistently in real-world texts; where the diaeresis is absent the modal (plain) vowel is emitted. (d) The map is dialect-neutral where the standard spelling is; Agar/Bor/Padang/Rek differences in vowel quality are not modelled.

ancestors.notes: Nilotic — the classification node this language sits under.

location.notes: Glottolog's computed centroid for the Dinka family — a summary of a whole clade's range, not a place anyone speaks.
